learning_outcomes.description: Explain foundational concepts and theories in criminology: Explain key criminological concepts, definitions, and major theoretical perspectives used to understand criminal behaviour.
Construct evidence-based criminological arguments: Develop clear, structured, and academically informed arguments supported by relevant research and data.
Find and synthesise criminological information: Locate, evaluate, and integrate information from diverse sources, including academic research, crime data, and media to inform criminological analysis.

Apply ethical approaches to criminological research and practice: Identify and critically assess ethical issues in criminological research and professional practice, including responsible use of AI and emerging technologies.
Demonstrate cultural competency in criminology: Recognise and appreciate how social diversity, inequality, and cultural contexts shape criminal behaviour and justice system responses.

assessments.task_description:
Due Date: Wednesday September 2nd 2026, 4pm (Week 6)

Word count: 1500 words (+/- 10%)

To complete Assessment 1 (Crime Fact Checking Investigation) and Assessment 3 (Theory Application Argument), you must choose a real crime incident or criminal case. You can find information in the Assessment folder on Blackboard Ultra on how to choose your crime incident.

Why have I assigned this assessment: Information in the 21st century is distributed and collected in a variety of ways. Being able to understand and judge the reliability and validity of data has never been more essential, particularly given that every person possess their own biases and these biases are reflected by machine learning. AI literacy is essential in modern criminology because artificial intelligence is increasingly being used in policing, courts, and corrections - often with profound implications for justice and equity.

Criminology students need to understand and recognise how AI systems can reproduce bias, how to competently verify AI-generated information, and how to critically evaluate algorithmic decision-making. This is not just about using technology; it is about understanding how the use of technology shapes crime, justice, and inequality. By developing AI literacy skills, students gain the analytical tools to work effectively in AI-mediated criminal justice systems while maintaining their commitment to fairness, accountability, and human dignity.

PART 1: Select your crime case/incident

Choose one crime incident that has generated public discussion. This could be:

  • A specific criminal case focusing on an individual (e.g., a high-profile robbery, assault case, fraud case, etc.)
  • A specific crime incident that involves more than one offender (e.g., 1999 WTO riot in Seattle, Cronulla race riot 2005, 2021 Capital riot, etc.)
  • A specific criminal case that involves institutional wrongdoing (e.g., Volkswagen emissions scandal, Purdue Pharma and the opioid epidemic, Theranos Scandal, etc.)

Requirements:

  • Must be from a credible news source (e.g., ABC News, The Guardian, BBC, local newspapers, etc.)
  • Must include a link to your news article in your submission
  • May include crime cases that have not been finalised
  • May include unsolved criminal cases
  • May include international incidents

Provide in your submission:

  • Case Name
  • Category/Classification of the Case
  • Link to your news article

PART 2: AI-Generated Summaries

Use two different AI tools/platforms (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Copilot, Gemini, etc.) to generate summaries of your chosen crime incident.

Prompt each AI with:

"Provide a detailed summary of [crime incident name/description] including what happened, who was involved, the criminal charges or outcomes, and any key facts about the case."

For each AI tool/platform, document:

  • Which AI tool you used
  • The exact prompt you used
  • Screenshot or copy of the AI's complete response

Provide an analysis of the outputs. In your response, compare what each AI stated. Consider the following:

  • How did the two AI summaries differ? How are they similar?
  • Which seemed more detailed or accurate at first glance?
  • Which output feels more accurate? Why? 

PART 3: Fact-Checking Claims

Now become a fact-checker! Select four claims from across the AI summaries and verify the selected claims.

Identify and fact-check at least 4 specific claims the AI made. For each claim include:

  1. The Claim (quote exactly what the AI said)
  2. Verification Decision:
  • TRUE - Accurate✓
  • ⚠️ PARTIALLY TRUE - Mostly accurate but missing nuance/context
  • ✗ FALSE - Incorrect or misleading
  • ? UNVERIFIABLE - Cannot confirm with available sources

3. Evidence:

  • What sources did you use to verify? (Provide links/citations you relied on in making your decision)
  • What did you find (True, partially true, false, or unverifiable)?
  • If the claim was false/misleading, provide the accurate information about this claim.
  • Why might the AI have gotten this wrong? If the claim was true, why might the AI have been able to accurately provide this information?

PART 4: Critical Reflection

Write a reflection and consider:

·        AI accuracy

·        Implications for research/industry

·        Your own assumptions/process/learning/reflections

Please see the task sheet on Blackboard for additional details.

Please note that reproductions of AI output do not count towards the total word count.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.

assessments.due_date:
10/08/2026 - 19/10/2026

Tutorial Exercises are held in Weeks 3 - 5, 7 - 9, and 11-12.
assessments.task_description:
Due Date: To be submitted at the end of the associated tutorial

Weighting: 40%

Tutorials in CRIM1000 are designed to do two things: Firstly, the exercises provide important information and direction in completing your assessments. Secondly, these exercises help you practice and develop the theory application and argument skills learned in the lecture and reading material each week.

During tutorials in Weeks 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 11, and 12, you will complete an interactive exercise. These exercises are based on analysing and responding to real-world crime scenarios presented through videos, case studies, artifacts, or news reports shown in class. You will work through these scenarios by applying criminological theories and concepts to explain criminal behaviour, identify contributing factors, and provide analytical responses.

These exercises are designed to help you practice critical thinking skills, engage with real-world crime scenarios, and demonstrate your understanding of lecture content and reading material through practical application. Explicit instructions on how each exercise works will be provided at the beginning of each tutorial.

Assessment Security Requirements:

These are secure, identity-verified assessments with the following requirements:

·        In-person attendance mandatory - Exercises can only be completed during your assigned tutorial.

·        No digital devices permitted - Computers, phones, tablets, or other digital devices cannot be used during the exercise – unless stated otherwise.

·        Paper-based responses only - You must complete responses on the paper materials provided by your tutor.

·        No external materials - Textbooks, notes, or outside materials are not permitted during the exercise.

·        No AI assistance - Artificial intelligence tools cannot be used to assist or complete these exercises.

·        Translation - Translation dictionaries are the only outside materials allowed.

Failure to abide by these conditions may result in an academic misconduct allegation.

Grading Structure:

There are 8 exercises offered over the course of the semester. You may attempt all of them; however, your best 5 exercise scores will contribute to 40% of your final grade. Each exercise is worth 8 points (Total possible: 40 points toward final grade).

Everything you need to complete each exercise will be provided to you in class.

Why does this assessment matter?

These interactive exercises are designed to help you apply theoretical knowledge to real-world crime scenarios in a supervised setting. They are designed to ensure that you are meeting the learning outcomes of this course and developing skills including critical thinking. This assessment gives you an opportunity to demonstrate what you have learned and build up confidence in theory application before your major assessment. In addition, these exercises help you practice exam-style thinking in a low-stakes environment while ensuring the academic integrity of your learning. 


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Worksheets must be submitted to your tutor at the end of the associated tutorial.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Due Date: Monday November 9th 2026, 4pm (Exam Week 1)

Word Count: 1200 words (+/- 10%)

HURDLE REQUIREMENT: You must submit a completed Generative AI Usage Statement (template provided in Blackboard). Even if you did not use AI to support your writing, you must still submit a completed Generative AI Usage Statement. Failure to do so will result in a grade of 0 for this assessment.

You will apply ONE criminological theory to explain the criminal behaviour in your chosen crime case (from the Crime Fact Checking Investigation assessment). Your submission includes TWO components: (1) a focused written argument explaining how your theory applies to the case, and (2) a visual pathway diagram mapping the theoretical explanation into the specific facts of the case.

Why have I assigned this task? Criminologists use theory to explain why crime occurs and why people offend. This assessment asks you to apply a theoretical framework to understand and explain a real world crime incident or criminal behaviour. The written component develops your ability to effectively construct an evidence-based argument, while the visual diagram helps you see and communicate the causal pathways that the theory proposes. Together, these skills are essential for anyone pursuing advanced criminological study and/or a career in criminal justice.

Task Description:

Part A: Written Component (1200 words - 25 marks)

Your written argument must answer the following question:

How does [insert chosen theory here] explain why [insert chosen offender here] engaged in [insert criminal behaviour here]?

OR

How does [insert chosen theory here] explain how [insert chosen incident here] occurred?

Your written argument should include:

-         A theory covered in the course and contained in the list provided on Blackboard

-         An explanation of the central principles or key elements of the theory and how these principles/elements come together in forming a criminal pathway

-         ONE example from peer-reviewed literature demonstrating where your chosen theory has been used in explaining similar cases/types of crime

-         An argument as to how your chosen theory explains the criminal behaviour in your chosen crime case

-         ONE critique of your chosen theory that is supported by evidence and peer-reviewed literature

This is not a formal essay so there is no need for an introduction or conclusion. Just focus on your argument.

PART B: Theory Pathway Diagram (15 marks)

On one A4 page, you will create a visual diagram of the causal pathway you are arguing is occurring to produce the criminal behaviour/incident. Examples of how to do this will be provided in lecture and in tutorials.

You may draw this pathway by hand (as long as it can be clearly read) or digitally create the diagram pathway. Your diagram must visually demonstrate HOW your case maps onto the causal pathway proposed by your chosen theory. You must include examples/evidence from your chosen crime case on the theoretical diagram.

Source Requirements:

  • A minimum of 4 scholarly, peer-reviewed references
  • Reliable sources about your chosen case (i.e. news reports, court documents, official reports, etc.)

Technical Requirements:

You may use AI to SUPPORT your writing. Tutorials will include a discussion of what appropriate AI usage looks like for this assessment.

This course prepares you to be able to have ethical and responsible conversations about the use of AI and also develop ethical practices when using AI. The Generative AI Use Statement is linked to this component of the class. Please note that you must submit a completed AI Use Statement regardless of whether you have used AI. Failure to do so will result in a 0 grade for this assessment.

The visual diagram, reference list, and AI statement are not included in the word count.

Please see the task sheet on Blackboard for additional details.
